meta.title: W203 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W203 engine — OM611, OM646, M111, M271, M112. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C-Class W203 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W203 engine typically costs £1,800–£6,500 supply only, depending on the engine — the volume M271 Kompressor sits at £2,200–£4,000, while the M112 V6 ranges from £3,000–£6,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W203 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646.962 diesel (C200/C220 CDI, 2003–2007) and the M112 V6 petrol (C240/C320) are the most dependable. The OM646 is an improved common-rail diesel with a proven 200,000-mile-plus lifespan, while the M112 V6 is smooth, robust, and free of major failure modes. The M271 Kompressor is also reliable if the supercharger and chain tensioner are maintained.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C-Class W203?

acceptedAnswer.text: M271 supercharger clutch and timing chain tensioner wear, OM611 injector seal failure ('Black Death'), M112 V6 balance shaft wear, and — unrelated to the engine — widespread rust and corrosion on pre-2004 facelift cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C-Class W203 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and the year. Post-2003 facelift cars with OM646 diesels or M112 V6 petrols are genuinely robust. Early cars (2000–2002) with M111 petrols or OM611 diesels are more prone to age-related issues. Rust is the W203's single biggest reliability concern regardless of engine.

mainEntity.name: Which W203 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the early M111.951/955 (C180, 2000–2002) and OM611.962 (C200/C220 CDI, 2000–2003) are the ones we'd advise caution on. The M111 suffers from camshaft adjuster oil leaks, while the OM611 is prone to injector 'Black Death'. If you buy one, budget for preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W203 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Supercharger clutch rattle or timing chain noise (M271), rough idle or diesel smell (OM611/OM646 injector failure), or balance shaft noise (M112 V6) are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C-Class W203 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000+ W203 (especially a C320 or low-mileage C220 CDI), a £3,000–£4,500 recon is 38–56% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,000 example with rust issues, a £3,000+ recon may exceed the car's worth — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C-Class W203 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646 diesel and M112 V6 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M271 Kompressor is typically good for 150,000+ miles if the chain and supercharger are maintained. The M111 and OM611 are the shortest-lived due to their known failure modes.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W203, S203 and CL203?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the second-generation C-Class platform and most engines. The W203 is the 4-door saloon (2000–2006, this page), the S203 is the Estate (2000–2007), and the CL203 is the 3-door SportCoupé (2001–2007). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C-Class W203 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., C220 CDI, C180 Kompressor, C320) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes C-Class W203 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W203 saloon — from the M111 petrol to the M271 Kompressor units, the OM611 and OM646 diesels, and the V6 petrols that defined Mercedes' second-generation compact executive. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the rust and balance-shaft issues that still haunt early cars. Backed by 252+ real UK W203 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C-Class W203 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W203 saloon specifically — from the early M111 four-cylinders to the M271 Kompressor units and the V6 petrol and diesel options that arrived with the 2004 facelift.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM611 and OM646 diesel families: 80% confidence — well-documented Mercedes diesel families with strong UK specialist support. M271 Kompressor: 80% confidence — proven supercharged four-cylinder family. M112 V6: 80% confidence — robust and well-understood. M111: 75% confidence — earlier four-cylinder with some known issues. All ratings are provisional pending dedicated Engine Pages.

overview.intro: The W203 is the second-generation C-Class, launched in July 2000 as the successor to the W202. It introduced a stiffer chassis, improved interior quality, and a more modern design language that echoed the larger S-Class. The engine range evolved significantly during production: early cars used the M111 2.0-litre four-cylinder and M112 V6 petrols, with OM611 and OM612 diesels. A major 2002 update replaced the M111 with the M271 Kompressor four-cylinders, while the 2004 facelift brought revised styling, the OM646 diesel, and later the M272 V6 petrols. A 2005 engine overhaul saw the C230K replaced by a V6, the C280 and C350 introduced, and the C320 CDI arriving at the expense of the C270 CDI.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W203 (shared with S203 estate, CL203 SportCoupé) • Predecessor: W202 • Successor: W204 • Sibling body styles in this generation: S203 Estate, CL203 SportCoupé.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W203 C-Class generated 252 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM611/OM646 diesels and M271 Kompressor petrols as these cars exit affordable-warranty age.

items.quote: The OM646 is the improved version of the OM611 — common-rail diesel with better injector reliability, improved emissions, and a proven 200,000-mile-plus lifespan. It's the W203's most dependable diesel, free of the earlier OM611's injector fragility.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a reliable, economical diesel without the V6's complexity.

items.quote: The 3.2-litre M112 V6 delivers 218bhp of smooth, naturally aspirated power. No supercharger to worry about, no timing-chain tensioner issues — just a robust, proven V6 that sounds wonderful and keeps going. The C240's 2.6-litre M112 is equally solid.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a smooth six-cylinder petrol without the Kompressor's complexities.

items.quote: The early 2.0-litre M111 four-cylinder is prone to camshaft adjuster magnet oil leaks that can reach the ECU, plus general age-related oil leaks from gaskets and seals. It's an old engine in an old car — and replacement costs frequently exceed the vehicle's value.
items.whoItsFor: budget buyers willing to accept age-related issues.

items.quote: The five-cylinder OM612 is rare and specialist — when the injectors fail or the turbo goes, parts are harder to find and labour is more intensive than the four-cylinder diesels. Replacement costs can hit five figures on a bad day.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need 170bhp diesel torque and are prepared for specialist maintenance.

items.quote: The 1.8-litre supercharged M271 offers 143bhp, decent economy, and the same fundamental robustness as its 163bhp sibling. It's the volume engine for good reason — cheap to run, cheap to replace, and more than enough for UK roads.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a premium badge without the premium running costs.

items.quote: The 2.6-litre M112 V6 is often overlooked in favour of the Kompressor fours or the bigger C320, but it offers 170bhp of smooth, naturally aspirated power without the M271's supercharger complexity. It's the W203's sleeper pick — a petrol that drives like a proper Mercedes.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a V6 without the C320's fuel consumption.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Early Petrol — budget option but ageing.
Smooth Six — V6 without the complexity.
Best Petrol — 218bhp V6, no turbo worries.
Best Diesel — the W203's most dependable.

economicsRule.title: The W203 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,800 OM646 recon against a £4,000 W203 sits at 63–120% of the car's value. On a sub-£4,000 W203, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example (particularly a C320 or C270 CDI), the same recon drops to a routine 31–60% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: M271 Kompressor Supercharger Clutch & Chain Tensioner
problems.rootCause: The M271's supercharger clutch can fail, causing loss of boost and a rattling noise. The timing chain tensioner is also a known weak point — chain stretch can lead to catastrophic failure if not addressed. Camshaft adjuster magnet oil leaks can reach the ECU.
problems.recommendation: On any W203 Kompressor over 60,000 miles, have the supercharger clutch and chain tensioner checked at every service — £500–£900 now is far cheaper than the £2,200–£4,000+ replacement that follows a skipped chain.

cta.label: Compare M271 engine prices for your W203 C180/C200 Kompressor →

problems.title: OM611 Injector Failure (Black Death)
problems.rootCause: The OM611's injector seals can fail, allowing combustion gases to leak past — this creates carbon buildup (the 'Black Death' effect) and eventually causes injector seizure and cylinder damage. The later OM646 improved this design significantly.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough idle or diesel smell in the cabin, get the injector seals inspected immediately — a £400–£800 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,800 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare OM611/OM646 engine prices for your W203 C200/C220 CDI →

problems.title: W203 Rust & Corrosion
problems.affectedModels: All W203 variants, particularly pre-2004 facelift cars.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the W203's most infamous issue — early cars suffer from significant rust on wheel arches, door bottoms, boot lids, and subframes. The 2004 facelift improved rust protection but didn't eliminate it entirely.
problems.recommendation: Have the wheel arches, door bottoms, and subframe inspected on any W203 — rust is the single most common reason an otherwise sound W203 fails an MOT. Pre-2004 cars are most at risk.

problems.title: M112 V6 Balance Shaft Wear
problems.rootCause: The M112 V6's balance shaft gear can wear, leading to timing issues and engine noise. This is more common on higher-mileage examples and can be expensive to fix.
problems.recommendation: If your M112 V6 develops a rattle or timing noise, get it inspected immediately — £600–£1,000 now is far cheaper than the £3,000+ replacement that follows a complete failure.

cta.label: Compare M112 engine prices for your W203 C240/C320 →

replacementCosts.h2: W203 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — W203 saloon shares identical engine-bay access with its S203 estate and CL203 SportCoupé siblings, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W203 saloon only. S203 Estate and CL203 SportCoupé share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
